Objectives

Understand what “agentic AI” is and why it’s different from chat assistants
Understand “workflow automation” as multi-step work, not one-off answers

Lessons (1–5)



What “Agentic AI” means (simp...
stage.languageDirective: Deliver the entire course in English, using clear and concise business and technology vocabulary suitable for adult learners new to the subject but with basic workplace experience. Define all key terms clearly on first use, and use plain English or brief explanations for any technical terminology. Keep AI and workflow automation terms in English with simple definitions. All scene content, slides, quizzes, and instructions must be in English; if any practical examples are drawn from web sources with non-English material, provide English explanations only.

elements.content: Course Introduction & Structure

Overview

From simple chatbots to agentic AI workflows

Course Structure

10 modules
Practicals & quizzes included

Learning Goals

Confident with agentic AI, ready for project capstone

Key terms explained:

• Agentic AI: AI systems that act with autonomy to complete tasks

• Workflow automation: Using technology to streamline business processes

actions.text: Hello everyone, and welcome! I’m Ms. Patel, and I’m excited to guide you through this course on Agentic AI and workflow automation. Let’s start by looking at our course introduction and structure.

First, here’s our course overview. We’ll go on a journey together: starting with the basics of chatbots, you’ll advance towards building agentic AI workflows—these are systems where AI can handle complex tasks from start to finish, not just simple questions.

Let’s talk about how the course is structured. There are 10 modules, each with a mix of practical exercises and quizzes. These activities are designed to make sure you not only understand the concepts, but can apply them in real workplace situations.

By the end of the course, you’ll feel confident using agentic AI and automating workflows, and you'll be ready for the capstone project—where you’ll put your new skills to work solving real-world problems.

Right away, let’s clarify some key terms we’ll use throughout. 'Agentic AI' refers to artificial intelligence systems that don't just follow simple instructions; they can act as agents, proactively managing a series of tasks, making decisions, and carrying out end-to-end workflows. Don’t worry—we’ll keep all definitions clear and straightforward as we go.

To sum up, our course will move from foundation to practical application, building your understanding and skills step by step. If you have any questions, please feel free to ask along the way. Now, let’s dive deeper into what Agentic AI is!

elements.content: What is Agentic AI?

Agentic AI

An AI system that can plan, decide, and act to achieve goals without step-by-step human guidance
vs

Traditional Chatbot

A system that responds only to single prompts or instructions—no full task planning

• Agentic AI automates multi-step tasks

• Saves time and reduces manual effort

• Enables more advanced workflows at work

actions.text: To start, let's clarify what we mean by 'Agentic AI'. This is the central concept you'll see throughout today's session.

Agentic AI refers to an AI system that can plan, make decisions, and take action on its own to complete tasks. In other words, it doesn't just give answers, but actually manages steps in a process independently.
It's important to understand how Agentic AI differs from the traditional chatbots you may have used before. Let's look at that comparison now.
A traditional chatbot is designed to just respond to specific requests or answer questions. It doesn't plan or keep track of long, multi-step processes—instead, it reacts one input at a time.

With Agentic AI, the real advantage is automation of complex, multi-step tasks. This kind of system can save a lot of time and help reduce repetitive work, allowing human workers to focus on more valuable activities.

To sum up: Agentic AI is more than a smart chatbot. It can handle planning and carrying out entire workflows, whereas a traditional chatbot only addresses individual questions. Understanding this distinction is crucial as we move on to see where Agentic AI can make a difference in your work.

elements.content: Chatbots vs Agentic Agents

Agentic Agent:
AI system that can use tools and plan steps

• Uses external tools
• Plans multiple steps
• More than one-turn answers

Chatbot

• Single-turn Q&A
• No tool use
• No task sequence

Agentic Agent

• Automates workflows
• Uses tools/resources
• Multi-step planning

actions.text: Now that we understand what agentic AI is, let's compare two common types of AI systems: classic chatbots and agentic agents.
First, let's look at chatbots. A chatbot is an AI that answers questions one at a time. This is called single-turn Q&A. It doesn't use external tools or resources, and cannot perform task automation.
In contrast, an agentic agent is an AI system with the ability to use tools and plan multiple steps. This means it can access external software, websites, or even send emails as part of its process.

Agentic agents can automate entire workflows by planning actions, using tools or resources, and tracking progress over several steps. This makes them far more powerful than classic chatbots, especially for business tasks.

For example, an agentic agent can automatically fill out a report, book meetings using your calendar, or find and send relevant documents. In summary, while chatbots simply answer questions, agentic agents can execute and manage real tasks from beginning to end.

elements.content: Understanding Workflows

• Workflow: a sequence of steps and handoffs

• Steps follow defined rules and order

• Handoffs move tasks between people or systems
Example: Simple workflow diagram

actions.text: Now, let's shift our focus to understanding workflows—one of the core building blocks for agentic AI in organizations.

A workflow is simply a sequence of steps that need to be followed to complete a task, including the handoffs between people or systems. In other words, a workflow defines the order in which things happen and who or what is responsible for each part.
Notice that effective workflows have clear rules. These rules help decide things like who approves a request, when to send an email, or what happens if an unusual situation comes up.
To make this more concrete, let's take a look at a simple workflow diagram example.

Here you can see an illustration of how steps in a workflow connect, including the handoffs from one part to another. This kind of diagram is widely used in workplaces to make processes clear and efficient.

In summary, workflows are just clearly defined steps and handoffs that help organizations get work done smoothly and consistently. Understanding and mapping these workflows is an essential starting point for successful automation with agentic AI.

elements.content: Where Agentic AI Adds Value

Automates Repetitive Tasks

E.g., data entry, scheduling, simple approvals

Supports Measurable Results

Tracks completion, accuracy, speed

Reduces Manual Workload

Frees time for decision-making or creative work

Workplace Examples

• Auto-generating standard reports

• Routing routine requests to the right person

• Monitoring compliance checklists

actions.text: Building on our understanding of workflows, let's explore where agentic AI can truly add value in the workplace.

First, agentic AI excels at automating repetitive tasks. These are activities that follow clear rules and need to be done often, like data entry or scheduling meetings. By handling these, AI makes daily work more efficient and frees up your time.

Next, agentic AI supports measurable results. This means it can track task completion, accuracy, or speed, allowing you to see the impact of automation in real numbers. Measurable outcomes make it simple to prove value to your team or management.
As a result, agentic AI reduces your manual workload. Instead of spending hours on routine tasks, you can focus more on decision-making and creative problem-solving.

Let's look at some workplace examples. Agentic AI can auto-generate standard reports, process invoices, manage service tickets, or handle routine email responses. These are areas where automation quickly shows benefit through speed and consistency.

To summarize, agentic AI creates the most impact when used for clearly defined, repetitive, and measurable tasks. Next, we'll learn how embracing the agent mindset can further boost efficiency and trust in automation.

elements.content: Adopting the Agent Mindset
Agent Mindset: Approach automation like assigning a task — focus on goals, steps, outputs, and limits

Set Clear Goals
What should the agent achieve?

Define Steps & Outputs
Outline actions and expected results

Add Constraints
Limits for safe, reliable outcomes

actions.text: Now, let's build on what we just discussed by looking at what it means to adopt the agent mindset. This approach is about thinking of automation as if you're assigning tasks to a capable team member — an agent — who needs clear direction, well-defined steps, and guardrails.

The agent mindset means viewing your automation as an agent: someone who doesn’t just follow a script, but actively works toward achieving your objectives. You need to set them up for success, just as you would a real colleague.
The first step is to set clear goals. Ask yourself, what exactly do you want the agent to achieve? Clear goals help avoid misunderstandings and ensure your automation stays on track.

Next, define the steps and expected outputs. This means outlining the actions the agent should take and what results you expect at each stage. A well-structured process makes the agent's actions predictable and easier to monitor.

Finally, it's essential to add constraints. These are limits and rules you put in place to keep the agent's behavior safe and reliable. Think of them as safety rails that prevent errors in your automation.

In summary, adopting the agent mindset means treating automation as you would a trusted colleague: provide clear goals, break the work into steps with expected outcomes, and set boundaries to ensure safe, reliable performance. This creates automations that are not only effective, but also easy to manage as your workflows grow more complex.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the main difference between a chatbot and an agentic AI (agent)?

options.label: A chatbot only responds to questions, while an agent can take actions to complete tasks.
A chatbot is always smarter than an agent.
An agent is limited to text conversations while a chatbot can connect to systems.
A chatbot and an agent are just different names for the same thing.

questions.analysis: Option A is correct because chatbots are designed mainly to respond conversationally, while agentic AI (agents) can not only reply but also initiate actions or automate tasks within workflows. Other options are incorrect because they misstate or oversimplify the differences.

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of value that agents can add to business workflows? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Automatically updating records across systems
Reminding users about upcoming deadlines
Engaging in open-ended human conversations for social purposes only
Triggering actions based on specified conditions

questions.analysis: The correct answers are A, B, and D. Agents add value by automating repetitive updates, sending reminders, and triggering actions, all of which streamline workflows. Option C is incorrect because agents are focused on work tasks, not just open-ended socializing.

questions.question: Which mindset is most important for working successfully with agentic AI in workplace automation?

options.label: A willingness to map out processes and spot where automation adds value
Memorizing every command the agent understands
Doing all tasks manually to ensure accuracy
Avoiding technology to reduce errors

questions.analysis: Option A is correct: Effective use of agentic AI requires a mindset open to identifying repeatable processes and opportunities for automation. Memorization and avoiding technology are not effective for leveraging agent value.

actions.text: Now let's check what we've learned about agentic AI, chatbots, and how automation adds value to business workflows.

For these questions, remember: a chatbot gives text responses to your questions, while an agentic AI—or agent—can also take actions in systems to actually complete tasks, not just provide information.

Another key point we just covered is that agents can add value by handling routine work, like automatically updating records, reminding you about upcoming deadlines, or triggering actions based on certain conditions. Watch out for options that only focus on casual conversation or things not related to business workflow automation.

When it comes to success with agentic AI, the most important mindset is to be willing to map out your processes, and look for places where automation can help. It's not about memorizing every tool or doing everything by hand, but about being open to spotting where AI can make your work easier and more efficient.

elements.content: The Automation Suitability Checklist
Automatable Task = A job or process a computer can do with little or no human help

Checklist

• Easy to repeat (same steps each time)

• Inputs are clear and well-defined

• Predictable outputs (consistent results)

Quick Tips:

• If a task follows clear rules, try automating it

• Start with short, routine processes for best results

actions.text: Now that we’ve seen where agentic AI can add value, let’s focus on how to decide which tasks are actually ready for automation. This page is all about the Automation Suitability Checklist.

First, let’s define what we mean by an 'automatable task.' It’s any job or process that a computer, like an intelligent agent, can perform instead of a person—without needing human judgment in every step.

Here are the main criteria: A task is a strong candidate for automation if it’s easy to repeat—meaning it can be done the same way each time. It should also have clear inputs, so you always know what information or materials you’re starting with, and predictable outputs, where the results are consistently what you expect when you follow the steps.
Notice how the checklist uses simple language. If you can easily write out the steps or see that there’s little variation from case to case, that’s a sign your process is ready for automation.

A quick tip: If a task follows clear rules—like 'if X happens, then do Y'—it’s almost always worth considering automation. Start with smaller, routine tasks before moving on to more complex processes.

To recap: use this checklist whenever you’re wondering if a task or process can be automated. Focusing on repeatability, clear inputs, and predictable outputs will help you spot the right opportunities and avoid frustration later.

elements.content: Time Audit: Finding Automation Gold
Time Audit: Tracking how much time is spent on your daily activities

Step 1: Spot time sinks (tasks taking lots of time)

Identify repetitive or manual tasks

Track daily and weekly activities

Step 2: Find automation candidates

Mark tasks for possible automation or delegation

actions.text: Let's continue by looking at 'Time Audit: Finding Automation Gold.' A time audit helps us discover hidden opportunities for automation by showing exactly how we spend our time each week, both personally and at work.
First, time audit means tracking how much time you actually spend on different tasks. This gives you real data, instead of guessing where your hours go.

Step one is to spot your time sinks. These are activities that take up a lot of your time, often more than you realize. Time sinks might include things like routine reporting, repeated approvals, or daily status updates.
Step two is to find automation candidates. Look for tasks on your audit list that follow the same steps each time or show clear, simple patterns. These are prime candidates for automation.
Here's a simple time audit chart to help visualize where your hours are going. Visual aids like this make it much easier to highlight problem spots and prioritize changes.
In summary, running a quick time audit can uncover tasks that are not only time-consuming, but also ready to be automated—helping you focus on higher-value work instead.

scenes.title: KPI Thinking: Speed, Errors, and Rework

elements.content: KPI Thinking: Speed, Errors, and Rework

Key KPIs

• Speed: How fast tasks are completed

• Error Rate: Mistakes found in outputs

• Rework: Tasks that must be corrected

• Turnaround: Total cycle time

Automation Impact

• Prioritize measurable results

• Focus on clear value changes
Time Saved > Effort to Maintain

actions.text: Now that we've explored how a time audit can reveal automation opportunities, let's dive into the key performance indicators—or KPIs—that help us measure the value of these changes. Our focus here is on KPIs like speed, errors, and rework.

First, let’s break down these KPIs. Speed measures how quickly a given task is completed. Error rate is simply the percentage of mistakes that occur in a process. Rework means the time or effort spent fixing those errors. Turnaround covers the full time from starting to finishing a task, including any corrections.

When considering automation, it's important to prioritize projects where you can see a clear, measurable improvement in these areas. In other words, choose tasks where automation will speed things up, reduce errors, or cut down on rework.

Here’s a simple formula to keep in mind: the time you save with automation should be greater than the effort it takes to maintain the solution. If you’re putting in more effort to maintain the automation than you’re actually saving, it’s time to step back and reconsider.
To sum up, always let these KPIs—speed, errors, and rework—guide your choices. They help ensure that automation delivers genuine, high-value results, not just extra complexity.

elements.content: Risk Rating: Human-First Tasks

Human-First Tasks

Critical impact, values, safety or trust needed

Risk Rating Steps

• Impact
• Harm potential
• Need for judgment

Avoiding Pitfalls

Review, oversight, keep humans in loop

Simple Risk Rating Scale:

• Low (automate OK) | • Medium (review needed) | • High (human-only)

actions.text: Now that we know how KPIs help us identify valuable automation, let's focus on a crucial question: which tasks must remain human-first? This means tasks where human judgment, oversight, or ethical consideration is absolutely essential.

Human-first tasks are those with critical impact on safety, values, or potential harm. These are areas where automation errors can be costly, dangerous, or even unethical. For example, making final hiring decisions or handling sensitive medical information.
To help us decide which tasks fit into this category, we use a risk rating framework. This framework scores tasks based on their impact, the potential for harm, and the need for strict oversight.

The simple risk rating scale shown here works like this: a rating of 'Low' means it's generally safe to automate, 'Med' means caution is needed and some human oversight is required, and 'High' means the task should remain human-first by default.

To avoid automation pitfalls, it's important to regularly review high-risk tasks, maintain oversight, and always keep humans involved in the decision process. This prevents unintended consequences and maintains trust.

In summary, not every workflow is suitable for full automation—especially those tied to safety, ethics, or high-impact outcomes. Using a simple risk rating framework helps us make smart, safe choices about where to keep humans in the loop.

questions.question: Which of the following is usually the most suitable for automation using workflow automation tools?

options.label: Processes with well-defined and repetitive steps
Tasks that change process steps frequently
Activities requiring complex human judgment
Projects with high creative demands

questions.analysis: Processes with well-defined and repetitive steps are most suitable for workflow automation since consistency and predictability increase reliability and reduce errors. Tasks with frequent changes or high creative and judgment demands are better handled by humans.

questions.question: Which of these statements are true when interpreting a time audit to pick tasks for automation? (Select all that apply)

options.label: High-volume repetitive tasks are good automation candidates
Tasks that are rarely performed should be prioritized for automation
Tasks causing significant bottlenecks in workflow are suitable for automation
Tasks already well-optimized by human workers always need automation

questions.analysis: High-volume repetitive tasks and those causing workflow bottlenecks are ideal for automation, as automation is best used where it can save significant time or remove obstacles. Rare or already optimized tasks usually offer little efficiency gain from automation.

questions.question: Which best describes how to balance risk and ROI (Return on Investment) when considering automation?

options.label: Prioritize only the ROI, ignoring risks entirely
Balance potential gains against possible implementation risks
Automate all possible tasks for maximum change
Avoid automating any process with any risk

questions.analysis: The best approach is to weigh financial and efficiency gains (ROI) against potential risks in implementation, ensuring net positive benefit for the organization. Focusing only on ROI, automating everything, or avoiding all risk leads to inefficient or unsustainable automation.

actions.text: Time for a quick quiz on the automation suitability checklist, time audit interpretation, and balancing risk with ROI—all topics we just covered.
For each question, read the options carefully. Remember: 'automation suitability' refers to whether a process is a good fit for being automated, not just whether it's possible.

Watch out for statements about tasks that rarely occur or those that require a lot of human judgment—these are often not the best automation candidates. Prioritizing high-volume, repetitive, or bottleneck tasks usually gives the greatest benefit.

And when it comes to ROI—Return on Investment—don't forget that balancing potential gains against the risks of automating is key. It's not just about maximizing change, but about making smart, worthwhile improvements.

elements.content: Mapping Workflows: Analyst Approach

Workflow Mapping Steps

1. Describe messy tasks (inputs, goals)

2. List all sub-tasks

3. Sequence steps from start to finish

4. Identify decision points and hand-offs

Flow Clarity

• Use clear labels for each step

• Show order and connections

Tips for Complex Work

• Break big steps into smaller actions

• Define each step in plain language

actions.text: Now, let’s focus on 'Mapping Workflows: Analyst Approach.' This section is about turning complicated, messy tasks into clear, agent-ready workflows.

The first step in workflow mapping is to describe the real, sometimes messy tasks we encounter in our daily work. Start by writing out the main actions, resources used, and people involved—don’t worry about making it perfect at this stage.

Next, bring clarity to the workflow by giving each step a clear label and connecting them in a way that makes the process understandable, even to someone new. Think of 'flow clarity' as making sure anyone can follow your map from start to finish without getting lost.
For complex tasks, the key is to break bigger steps into smaller, manageable pieces. This helps ensure the agent understands each part of the workflow and doesn't skip important details.

To recap: mapping workflows involves describing real work as it happens, making each step clear and easy to follow, and breaking down complexity for agents to execute reliably. These skills will be essential as we design effective agentic processes in the coming sections.

elements.content: Inputs, Outputs, and Handoffs

Key Terms

• Input: What is needed for the task

• Output: The result you expect

• Handoff: When you pass the work

• Identify all required inputs

• Define expected outputs clearly

• Document each handoff point

actions.text: Now that we’ve looked at the essentials of mapping workflows, let's focus on a foundational step: understanding inputs, outputs, and handoffs. This is a key building block for clear and reliable processes.

First, let's define these terms clearly. An input is what you need before a task can start—resources, information, or approvals. Outputs are the results produced by a task. Handoffs are the moments a task or information is passed from one person or system to another. Clear definitions help avoid confusion later on.

When you’re mapping a workflow, always identify all the required inputs at each stage, and clarify what a successful output looks like. Documenting expected outputs helps everyone know when a task is truly complete.

Here’s a visual that highlights where handoffs typically happen within a workflow. Each arrow or box can represent a point where one team or system hands control or information to another. These handoff points can be a common source of delay or error if not mapped out explicitly.

In summary, by clarifying inputs, outputs, and handoffs, you set a clear foundation for designing agentic workflows that run efficiently and reduce mistakes. On the next page, we’ll discuss rules and exceptions, which are just as important for making workflows agent-ready.

elements.content: Rules, Judgment, and Exceptions

Key Terms

• Rules: Clear IF/THEN instructions
• Judgment: Human decisions based on context
• Exceptions: Outliers needing special handling
Steps requiring human judgment stay with people
Common exceptions: unusual situations, errors, or policy overrides

Practical Steps

• Identify what can be rules vs. where judgment is needed

• Note common exceptions and solutions

• Write clear IF/THEN documentation for rules

actions.text: Now that we've seen how automation can increase efficiency, let's focus on an essential part of workflow design: rules, judgment, and exceptions.

Here are some key terms. First, 'rules' are clear IF/THEN instructions, which means they're step-by-step guidelines a system can follow without needing any special interpretation. 'Judgment' involves situations that require a person to make a decision based on their understanding, experience, or context.

When steps require human judgment, they should remain with people. For example, evaluating an unusual request or interpreting a policy in a gray area are tasks best handled by humans rather than automated agents.

Now let's look at exceptions. These are common in workflows and refer to unusual situations, errors, or unexpected events that don't fit standard rules. Handling exceptions often relies on human intervention to prevent problems or address unique cases.

For practical steps, it's important to document what can be handled by rules and what requires human judgment. Start by identifying which parts of a process can be clearly defined with IF/THEN logic, and mark those steps that should be left to people for careful consideration.
In summary, by sorting tasks into rules, judgment, and exceptions, you can design workflows that play to both human strengths and automation capabilities.

content.html:
Workflow Mapping Interactive Diagram

Purpose:
Use this interactive flowchart to learn how to map a simple business workflow, identify process steps, recognize decision points, and visualize team handoffs.

Key Points

  • Map process steps clearly from Start to Finish
  • Add Decision Points and possible Exceptions
  • Show Team Handoffs at each stage
Glossary:
Workflow: A repeatable series of tasks and decisions to achieve a business result.
Step: A distinct activity within a workflow.
Decision Point: A stage where an outcome depends on a Yes/No or choice.
Handoff: When responsibility moves from one team or role to another.
Exception: When the process goes off the usual path due to error or special case.

nodes.label: Start
nodes.details:
Start
This is where the workflow begins. Identify the entry trigger for this process, such as a customer request or internal need.

nodes.label: Gather Information
nodes.details:
Step: Gather Information
Collect all data and documents required to proceed.

Team: Operations
Tip: Standardize inputs for consistency.

nodes.label: All Info Complete?
nodes.details:
Decision Point
Review if all required info has been received. If not, return or escalate.

nodes.label: Review Submission
nodes.details:
Step: Review Submission
Assigned team member checks the accuracy and completeness.
Team: Quality Assurance

nodes.label: Approve?
nodes.details:
Decision Point
Decide if the request meets criteria for approval.
If not, reject and notify submitter.

nodes.label: Exception Handling
nodes.details:
Exception
If info is incomplete or incorrect, this step handles corrections or follow-up.
Team: Customer Service

nodes.label: Process Request
nodes.details:
Step: Process Request
Carry out the main business action (e.g., fulfill order, resolve issue).
Team: Fulfillment

nodes.label: Finish
nodes.details:
Finish
This is the end of the workflow. Typically includes completion confirmation or outcome reports to relevant teams or customers.

teacherActions.content: Let’s learn how to design a simple workflow map, showing steps, decision points, exceptions, and how teams hand off tasks.
teacherActions.label: Introduction

teacherActions.content: Every workflow begins with a trigger or starting point, like a customer request. This is the 'Start' node.
teacherActions.label: Show Start

teacherActions.content: 'Gather Information' is the first step. Here, the Operations team collects data needed for the process.
teacherActions.label: Show Gather Info

teacherActions.content: This is a decision point. Here, the team checks if all required information is complete before moving on.
teacherActions.label: Show Decision

teacherActions.content: This handoff shows work moving from the Operations team to Quality Assurance. Visualizing handoffs helps clarify team responsibilities.
teacherActions.label: Highlight Handoff

teacherActions.content: If information is missing or incorrect, the process moves to this exception handling step for corrections.
teacherActions.label: Show Exception

teacherActions.content: Finally, all workflows should have a clear finish, where results are confirmed or reported.
teacherActions.label: Show Finish

questions.question: Which of the following best describes workflow mapping?

options.label: Creating a visual step-by-step representation of a business process, showing the sequence of tasks, roles, inputs, and outputs.
Listing all tasks within a department in no particular order.
Assigning all tasks to a single individual.
Outlining only the exceptions in a process flow.

questions.analysis: Option A is correct because workflow mapping means providing a clear, visual sequence of the steps, people involved, inputs, and outputs in a process. Options B and D are incomplete or focus only on isolated elements. Option C ignores best practice for roles and responsibility distribution.

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of 'handoffs' in a workflow? (Select all that apply)

options.label: A manager reviewing and approving an employee's report before it is sent to a client.
An automated system assigning customer support tickets to available agents.
An employee working alone to complete a task from start to finish.
A completed form being passed from human resources to payroll for processing.

questions.analysis: A, B, and D are correct because a handoff occurs whenever inputs are transferred between people, teams, or automated systems. Option C is not a handoff since it does not involve passing work between parties.

questions.question: When mapping a workflow, which are important steps for handling exceptions (unexpected events or errors)? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Documenting who is responsible for investigating errors when they occur.
Ignoring exceptions to simplify the workflow diagram.
Describing what triggers an exception handling process.
Defining methods to communicate and resolve exceptions.

questions.analysis: A, C, and D are correct because handling exceptions means specifying roles, triggers, and communication methods to address errors or unusual cases. B is incorrect as ignoring exceptions undermines workflow reliability.

actions.text: Let’s take a quick check on what we just covered about workflow mapping, identifying handoffs, and handling exceptions.

Read each question carefully. Remember, 'workflow mapping' means creating a visual, step-by-step map that shows the sequence of tasks, the people or roles involved, the inputs (materials or info coming in), and the outputs (results going out).
For 'handoffs', think about whenever work passes from one person, team, or system to another. These are often places where mistakes can happen, so it’s important to spot these in your workflow.

When handling exceptions, or unexpected events and errors, it’s best practice to document who is responsible, what triggers the exception process, and how communication about the problem happens. Simply ignoring errors makes your workflow less reliable.

elements.content: Agent 'Brain', Tools, and Memory

• Agent 'brain': holds plan, goals, and instructions

• Tools: actions used to complete tasks

• Memory: store facts and results for future use

• Forget temporary details after tasks are done

actions.text: Now, let's shift our focus to how an agent's 'brain', the tools it uses, and memory all work together to drive agentic AI.

First, the agent's 'brain' is like its command center—it holds the agent’s plan, its goals, and any instructions it needs to follow. Think of this as the AI’s decision-making hub, setting priorities and deciding what steps to take.

Let’s compare the agent brain, tools, and memory in detail. This table breaks down their purpose and gives simple examples. Notice how each plays a unique role: the brain sets direction, tools carry out actions, and memory keeps important information for future use.

Finally, let's talk about memory. In agentic AI, memory means storing facts and results so the agent can use them again later. But it's also important for the agent to decide what information to keep and what to forget—holding onto too much can slow things down or cause confusion.

To sum up: an agentic AI makes progress with its brain setting goals and plans, tools taking action, and memory ensuring that learning builds up over time without getting clogged by unnecessary details. Understanding these parts will help when we move into planning and human checkpoints next.

elements.content: Planning and Human Checkpoints

Break Down Tasks

Divide complex work
into smaller actions

Human-in-the-Loop

Insert checkpoints
for review or approval

Balance

Mix automation
with oversight

• Task breakdown: Split large jobs into steps an AI agent or a person can check off

• Human checkpoint: Critical approvals, quality reviews, decision points

• Goal: Reliable workflow blending automation with human control

actions.text: Now that we've mapped out workflow steps, let's talk about how to plan agent tasks and identify where human checkpoints fit in. The key here is balancing what agents can do independently with moments where human approval or review is needed.
First, break down large and complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps. This is known as task breakdown. By dividing the work, you give the agent clear actions, reducing confusion and errors.
Think of this like splitting a big project into a checklist—each item can be handled on its own, making it easier to monitor progress and quality.

Next, insert human-in-the-loop checkpoints. This means deliberately choosing moments where a person needs to review, approve, or adjust the agent’s work. For example, before sending an important client email, you might require a manager to check it.
Balancing autonomy with oversight is critical. Too many checkpoints slow things down, but too few can lead to mistakes slipping through. The right mix keeps agent-driven workflows efficient yet safe.

In summary, always break down tasks clearly, build in thoughtful human reviews, and balance automation with practical oversight. These strategies ensure your agent workflows are both productive and reliable.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the main purpose of the 'agent brain' in an AI agent system?

options.label: It stores previous interactions for future use
It chooses actions, makes decisions, and guides the agent’s workflow
It provides connections to external applications or services
It gives human users access to the agent’s log files

questions.analysis: Option B is correct because the 'agent brain' controls the main logic, decision making, and workflow steps of the agent. Option A refers to 'memory', option C refers to 'tools', and option D is unrelated to core agent structure.

questions.question: Which of the following are important reasons to include checkpoints for human approval in an agent’s planning process? (Select all that apply)

options.label: To ensure tasks align with business goals before execution
To allow humans to manually program the agent's 'brain'
To let users review and adjust the agent’s planned steps as needed
To reduce the risk of the agent taking unintended actions

questions.analysis: Options A, C, and D are correct because human approval helps ensure that agent activities match business goals, allows adjustment of plans, and minimizes risks of undesired outcomes. Option B is incorrect as human approval is not for manual programming but for oversight and corrective action.

actions.text: Now let's check your understanding with a quick knowledge check on agent structure and planning. Answer each question carefully — and select all that apply where prompted.

Remember, in an AI agent system, the 'brain' is the central part that makes decisions, chooses actions, and directs the agent’s workflow. The agent's 'tools' allow it to interact with external services, while its 'memory' stores information from past actions.

For the second question, it's important to know why human checkpoints matter: they ensure tasks match business goals, let people adjust agent plans, and help prevent unintended actions. One common mistake is thinking human checkpoints are only about programming or technical setup — they're actually about strategic oversight.

elements.content: Designing Agentic Workflows
Key steps for building reliable agentic AI workflows

Trigger:
• Time (scheduled)
• Event (change, alert)
• Request (user asks)

Sequencing:
• Action order
• Confirmation steps
• Clear completion criteria

Branching:
• If/else logic
• Escalate to human
• Monitor for 'done'

actions.text: Now that we've seen where human checkpoints fit in agentic workflows, let's move on to the nuts and bolts of designing those workflows.
These are the key steps for building a reliable agentic AI workflow. We'll look closely at what triggers the workflow, how actions are sequenced, and how branching and escalation are handled.

First, let's talk about triggers. A trigger is what starts the workflow. It could be scheduled time, an event like a data update or alert, or even a direct request from a user. Knowing exactly what should kick off the process sets the foundation for a predictable, robust workflow.

Next, sequencing determines the order of actions. This means setting up each step the agent should follow, adding any confirmation steps, and making sure everything is done cleanly and correctly. Sequencing is about ensuring tasks run smoothly, without missed steps.

Finally, branching introduces decision points. This is where if/else logic comes in—if a task can't be completed or an exception occurs, the workflow could escalate to a human or start a monitoring process. Establishing clear branching means the agent won't be stuck or confused by unexpected situations.

To recap, a well-designed agentic workflow has clearly defined triggers, thoughtfully sequenced actions, and solid branching for when things get off track. These steps help ensure your agent is reliable and effective in real-world scenarios.

scenes.title: From Trigger to Close: Steps in Workflow Design

elements.content: From Trigger to Close: Steps in Workflow Design

Step Order

Arrange tasks in the proper sequence for a reliable workflow

Completion Signals

Use clear evidence (data or status) to confirm each step is finished

Handoffs & Closing

Transfer tasks smoothly and ensure all requirements are checked

• Trigger: Event that starts the workflow

• Step: Individual task or action in sequence

• Completion Evidence: Proof a step is done

actions.text: Now, let's walk through the main steps in workflow design, from the initial trigger to closing out the process.

First, you see the Trigger—which is the event that kicks off the workflow, such as receiving a new order or a customer request. Each action that follows is a Step, meaning an individual task the workflow must complete. Let’s remember: a workflow is just a set of repeatable steps for a job, often involving both people and automation.
Once you identify the steps, it's critical to arrange them in the proper sequence. Designing a robust step order ensures every task happens in the right moment, without confusion or gaps.

Next, let's look at completion signals. These are the clear pieces of evidence—like data entries or system notifications—that show a task is done and ready for the next one. Reliable signals help agents and coworkers avoid mistakes or delays.

Finally, we need to focus on handoffs and closing. This means smoothly transferring work from one person or system to another, making sure nothing falls through the cracks, and confirming everything is complete before closing the workflow.

To recap: every effective workflow starts with a clear trigger, follows a logical sequence of steps, uses solid completion signals, and ends with strong handoffs and closure. These principles help your agentic AI or automation deliver reliable results every time.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a trigger in workflow automation?

options.label: To start a workflow when a specific event occurs
To evaluate task completion criteria
To notify users about workflow errors
To archive completed workflows automatically

questions.analysis: A trigger is an event that starts or initiates a workflow process. Evaluating completion criteria, sending notifications, and archiving workflows are important but handled by other workflow steps or system rules.

questions.question: Which of the following best describe good practice for designing 'done' criteria in a workflow? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Clearly define what makes each step complete
Allow for ambiguous completion steps
Use confirmations or checks where needed
Skip defining output for steps that handoff to another team

questions.analysis: Good 'done' criteria should be clear and include confirmation mechanisms where needed. Ambiguity or skipping outputs increases the risk of errors and delays in handoffs.

actions.text: Now let's check what we've learned so far about workflow triggers and how to define when each step is 'done'.
Read each question carefully. Remember, a trigger is what starts a workflow based on a specific event, like a new task assignment.

For the second question, focus on what makes good 'done' criteria. Each workflow step should have clear, specific definitions for completion, including confirmations or checks where appropriate. Avoiding ambiguity is important, especially when steps are handed off to other teams.
Common mistakes include forgetting to clearly define completion signals or skipping checks when a task is handed off. These gaps can lead to confusion or missed steps in automation.

elements.content: Input Grounding and Trusted Sources

Grounding
Using only reliable, verified sources for input

Messy Inputs
Untrusted or unclear data increases errors

Trust Lists
Set up ‘trusted’ and ‘do not use’ input lists
Definition: Input grounding means only letting agents use data from sources you trust.
Tip: Review input sources often to keep your trusted list up to date.

actions.text: Now, let's focus on 'Input Grounding and Trusted Sources'. This concept is essential for ensuring that our AI agents are working with information we can actually rely on.
First, let’s define input grounding. That means allowing our agent only to use data from sources we trust, filtering out anything that’s unverified or unclear.
Grounding is all about using reliable, verified sources. When we set up an agent, making sure it pulls information only from dependable inputs helps reduce mistakes and keeps results consistent.
If the system receives messy or untrusted data, errors quickly follow. Just like in business processes, unreliable information can lead to confusion or even risky decisions.

To prevent this, we create lists—one for trusted inputs and another for sources that the agent should never use. This way, we’re setting clear boundaries so the agent knows exactly where to find good information.
And here’s a practical tip: regularly review your input sources so your trust lists stay up to date. It’s important to adjust as your business evolves or as new risks are identified.

In summary, by insisting on input grounding and using clear trusted source lists, we lay a solid foundation for error-proof automation. Next, we'll address how to manage sensitive documents and boundary settings for agents.

elements.content: Documents, Permissions, and Sensitive Boundaries
Key Concepts

Document Workflows

• Summarize
• Extract
• Classify

Permission Mindset

• Decide who can access
• Limit info by role
• Just enough access

Sensitive Boundaries

• Never include passwords
• Exclude private data
• Avoid trade secrets in prompts

actions.text: Now, let's focus on how documents, permissions, and sensitive boundaries play a critical role in agentic AI workflows. These factors help ensure automation operates safely and responsibly.

First, consider document workflows. This means understanding what it looks like to summarize, extract, or classify documents within an automated process. Summarizing captures the main idea, extracting pulls out selected data, and classifying groups documents by their characteristics.

Next, let's talk about the permission mindset. At its core, this is about deciding who should have access to which documents and limiting unnecessary exposure. By controlling permissions, we minimize the risk of sensitive information being seen or used incorrectly. It's not just a technical setting—it's a habit of thinking about data safety at every step.

Finally, setting sensitive boundaries is essential. This means never including items like passwords or highly confidential information in any prompts or documents an agent can access. By defining clear boundaries, we protect our data and avoid accidental leaks or misuse.

To summarize, start with well-defined workflows, remember to apply a permission mindset, and always respect sensitive boundaries. Practicing these makes agentic AI safer and more effective for everyone involved.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a trusted source when grounding AI inputs in a business context?

options.label: A peer-edited website open to public contributions
An official internal company policy document with current permissions
A collection of opinions from online forums
A document with no clear author or publication date

questions.analysis: Option B is correct because official internal company documents that are up-to-date and have appropriate permissions are considered trusted sources in workplace settings. The other options may lack authority, accuracy, or verification.

questions.question: Which of the following are good practices for ensuring document quality and permission when using sources in automated workflows? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Verifying the document's author and publication date
Using documents marked as confidential without authorization
Checking if the document is the latest version
Ensuring you have permission to access and share the document

questions.analysis: A, C, and D are correct because verifying author and date, using the latest version, and ensuring proper permissions are key steps for quality and compliance. B is incorrect: using confidential documents without authorization violates company policy.

actions.text: Now let's check what we've learned about grounding AI inputs and safe document use in workflows.

For the first question, think about what makes a source 'trusted' in a business context. Trusted sources are official, up-to-date, and come from recognized authorities, like internal policy documents. Avoid using sources that are unverified, allow anonymous changes, or lack clear authorship.

For the second question, you're asked to select all good practices that help ensure document quality and permission. These include verifying details like the author and publication date, making sure the document is the most current version, and only using or sharing documents when you have the right permissions. Never use confidential information without proper authorization.
Remember, grounding your AI's inputs in reliable information and respecting privacy boundaries is essential for safe and effective automation, just as we covered on the previous pages.

elements.content: Testing & Quality Control for Agents
Key Terms & Steps

• Output validation: check results before actions

- Pass/Fail rule: Only continue if check is passed

• Two-pass evaluation: draft, verify, then act

- Improves accuracy by reviewing before final step

• Monitoring: track logs & review performance regularly
Definitions: Validation = checking if outputs make sense. Monitoring = reviewing results and process quality over time.

actions.text: Now, let's move on to how we test and ensure quality control for agents. This is a crucial part of building reliable automation.
We'll start by breaking down the key terms and steps involved in testing agentic systems, so you have a practical checklist for real-world applications.

Let's define an important term: 'Validation' means checking if an agent's output makes sense before moving on to the next step in the workflow. In other words, does the result look correct or logical?

A simple approach is output validation—before an agent takes action, you check if its result passes or fails your standards. Think of this as a quick review to prevent errors before they become bigger problems.

The next key step is two-pass evaluation: first, the agent drafts a response, then it verifies that draft before acting. This adds an extra layer of safety, ensuring that only high-quality outputs are used.
Lastly, regular monitoring is essential—this means reviewing logs and keeping a scheduled check on agent performance, so problems are caught early and fixed quickly.

In summary, testing and quality control involve validating outputs, using a two-step evaluation process, and continuously monitoring your agents. This process ensures reliability and builds trust in automation. Next, we'll see how to track common quality issues and define effective KPIs.

elements.content: Common Failure Modes & Quality KPIs

Common Failure Modes

• Incorrect facts

• Missing steps or details

• Outdated responses

• Incomplete follow-up

Quality KPIs

• Accuracy: correct facts and actions

• Turnaround: speed to complete tasks

• Rework Rate: % needing corrections

Tips for Tracking & Improvement

- Measure KPIs regularly

- Review errors and address root causes

- Use feedback for continuous improvement

actions.text: Let’s continue by exploring common failure modes for agentic workflows, as well as the key performance indicators, or KPIs, we use to measure quality.

First, common failure modes. These are typical mistakes that agents might make. They include giving incorrect facts or missing important steps in a workflow. Both can impact the reliability of automation, so it's important to track when and why they happen.

Next, let’s look at Quality KPIs. KPIs, or Key Performance Indicators, are measurable values that help us understand how well our agentic workflows perform. Accuracy means the agent gives correct facts and carries out the right actions. Rework rate tracks how often errors need to be fixed or tasks need to be repeated. Turnaround time looks at how fast a task is completed. Monitoring these KPIs helps us spot issues quickly.

For practical improvement, it’s useful to measure these KPIs regularly. By setting up simple tracking, you can see trends over time, identify areas that need attention, and make steady improvements. Even a basic spreadsheet can help spot repeated errors or slow steps.

So in summary, knowing what can go wrong and measuring key indicators helps us maintain high-quality agentic workflows. Up next, we'll check your understanding before introducing more advanced topics.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a validation rule in the context of agent workflows?

options.label: A set of criteria used to check that input data meets required standards before processing
A report showing all completed agent actions
An indicator of how quickly an agent finishes a task
A notification sent to users at the end of a process

questions.analysis: Option A is correct because a validation rule defines the required standards or conditions that input data must meet to continue in a process. The other options describe unrelated concepts such as reports (B), performance timing (C), and notifications (D).

questions.question: Which of the following are common error types or quality control indicators found in agent validation processes? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Missing required field
Duplicated entry
Incorrect password
Process complete notification

questions.analysis: Options A, B, and C are all common examples of validation errors or quality control indicators: missing required input, duplicate records, and input errors such as incorrect passwords. Option D refers to a workflow notification, not a validation error or quality indicator.

actions.text: Now let's check what we've learned about agent validation and quality control. Please read each question carefully and select the best answers based on what we just covered.

Remember, a validation rule is simply a set of criteria used to make sure input data is correct and meets required standards before it gets processed. This is an essential quality control tool in any agent workflow.
Common errors flagged by validation include missing required fields and duplicated entries. These help us catch mistakes early, which is a key part of maintaining reliable workflows.
Make sure you don't confuse process notifications or speed indicators with validation rules or error types—they serve different purposes in agentic systems.

scenes.title: Governance and Guardrails for Safe Scaling

elements.content: Governance and Guardrails for Safe Scaling

1. Governance

Rules, processes, and accountability for safe AI at scale

2. Guardrails

Allowed vs. forbidden actions
Prevent unsafe or off-policy behavior

3. Audit & SOPs

Document actions
Standard Operating Procedures for traceability
Note: Scaling AI agents increases risk. Governance and guardrails help maintain control and compliance.

actions.text: Now, let's focus on Governance and Guardrails for Safe Scaling—essential parts of deploying agentic AI at a larger scale. As you increase the number of automated agents, risks and challenges grow, so strong oversight becomes even more important.

First, governance refers to the rules, processes, and accountability structures placed around your AI agents. In simple terms, governance is about who is responsible for what, and how decisions are made to manage risks as your system grows.

Next, guardrails set clear boundaries for what agentic AI is allowed—and not allowed—to do. Guardrails help prevent mistakes, misuse, and unwanted behavior by defining forbidden actions and providing checks before risky steps.

Equally important is audit-ready documentation and a strong Standard Operating Procedure, or SOP, mindset. This means consistently recording agent actions and establishing clear step-by-step procedures, so anyone can review or reproduce what an agent did and why.
Keep in mind that scaling agentic AI introduces additional risk. Solid governance and well-defined guardrails keep your organization safer even as you automate more tasks.

To recap, as you scale up agentic AI, focus on governance for decision-making and accountability, guardrails for safety, and thorough documentation so issues can be traced or prevented. These steps lay the foundation for trustworthy and effective automation.

elements.content: Approvals, Least Privilege, and Escalation

Approval Gates

• Required for high-risk actions
• Prevent unintended changes

Least Privilege

• Give only necessary access
• Reduces risk and exposure

Escalation Plans

• Process for urgent issues
• Supports safety and compliance

actions.text: Now let's move on to three critical controls for safe agentic automation: approvals, the principle of least privilege, and escalation plans.

First, let's focus on approval gates. An approval gate means that specific actions—especially high-risk or sensitive steps—can't proceed without sign-off from the right person. This prevents mistakes or unauthorized changes from affecting important systems.

Next is the least privilege principle. This means agents—and people—should only get the minimum access needed to do their tasks. By reducing unnecessary permissions, we limit the opportunity for accidents or security problems if something goes wrong.

Finally, let's look at escalation plans. An escalation plan is a defined process for handling urgent issues or exceptions. It specifies who to notify and what steps to take if an agent encounters a problem it can't resolve safely. This keeps the workflow controlled, even under unexpected conditions.
To sum up, using approval gates, least privilege, and proper escalation gives us strong safety and compliance guardrails—all essential as we scale automation with confidence.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of implementing guardrails and governance rules when deploying agentic workflows in an organization?

options.label: To ensure that automated agents operate within defined safety boundaries
To increase the speed of workflow automation
To reduce the amount of human input required in all processes
To maximize costs for IT support

questions.analysis: Option A is correct because guardrails and governance rules are put in place to make sure that automated agents act within pre-set safety and compliance boundaries, minimizing risks. The other options are incorrect: speed and human input may be affected by governance, but are not the main purpose, and maximizing costs is never the goal.

questions.question: Which of the following are typically included in approval and escalation procedures for safe agentic workflow deployments? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Specifying criteria for when automated actions require human approval
Clear documentation of decision-making steps
Automated escalation to supervisors if an action goes beyond assigned permissions
Allowing agents to override any restriction for workflow speed

questions.analysis: A, B, and C are correct as approval and escalation procedures should define when a human must approve an action, ensure actions are documented for transparency, and trigger escalation to supervisors as needed. Allowing agents to override restrictions for speed (D) contradicts the goal of controlled, safe deployment.

actions.text: Now let's check what we've learned about governance and guardrails for agentic workflow deployments. Please read each question carefully and select the best answer.

Remember, governance rules help keep automated agents working safely within boundaries—just like company policies guide human employees. Guardrails are safety measures set up to prevent agents from making choices that could cause harm, break rules, or bypass important approvals.

When you see questions about approval and escalation procedures, think about steps like requiring human sign-off for risky actions, documenting decision paths clearly, and ensuring agents can't overstep their permissions without oversight.

A common mistake is thinking the main goal of these rules is speed or reducing human involvement, but actually, they exist to balance automation with control and safety. Take your time to choose answers that focus on safety and proper oversight.

elements.content: Ready-to-Use Workflow Playbooks
Practical Examples for Everyday Business Tasks

Step-by-step example:
Email workflow

Other scenarios:
Meetings, support, sales, HR

Template packs
for quick adoption
Playbook: A reusable guide or set of steps for completing a workflow using automation tools.

actions.text: Now that we've discussed safety and proper oversight, let's shift our focus to practical tools you can start using right away—specifically, ready-to-use workflow playbooks.

First, let's define what a playbook means in this context: a playbook is a reusable guide or set of steps that walks you through a specific process, making it easier to get repeatable results each time.
A practical example is an email workflow playbook. This playbook gives you a step-by-step process for automating and managing common email tasks, so you don't have to reinvent a process every time.
But workflow playbooks aren't just for email—they also cover other everyday business scenarios, including meetings, customer support, sales activities, and human resources tasks.

To make adoption even faster, template packs are available. These are collections of pre-made playbooks that you can adapt for your team, helping you get started quickly without creating processes from scratch.
All these examples are focused on everyday business tasks, showing how agentic AI and workflow automation can simplify your routine and free up time for more important work.
In summary, ready-to-use workflow playbooks provide clear, repeatable steps for common office processes. They're practical tools designed to help you start leveraging automation right away.

content.html:
Build Your Pilot Workflow Pack
Interactively guide learners through creating a pilot workflow pack for their own scenario using provided templates.
Step 1: Choose a real-world scenario
Scenario: Select a practical work process or challenge you want to improve or automate.

Definition: A scenario is a description of a specific situation or process in your workplace. For example, "Onboarding a new employee" or "Filing monthly expense reports."

nodes.label: Choose a real-world scenario
nodes.details:
Scenario: Select a practical work process or challenge you want to improve or automate.

Definition: A scenario is a description of a specific situation or process in your workplace. For example, "Onboarding a new employee" or "Filing monthly expense reports."

nodes.label: Fill out a workflow using template
nodes.details:
Workflow Template: Use the provided template to describe each step of your scenario. Include who is responsible, what actions are needed, inputs, and expected outcomes.

Definition: A workflow is a sequence of steps carried out to complete a process. A template is a structured document that guides you to provide details consistently.

nodes.label: Review and refine your pack
nodes.details:
Review & Refine: Evaluate your workflow pack for clarity and completeness. Adjust any unclear steps, verify responsibilities, and ensure outcomes are measurable.

Definition: A workflow pack is a set of prepared documents and instructions describing a business process, intended for testing or pilot use.

teacherActions.content: Let's walk through how to create your own pilot workflow pack using these three steps.
teacherActions.label: Introduction

teacherActions.content: First, select a real-world scenario from your workplace. This could be a process you'd like to improve or automate.
teacherActions.label: Highlight scenario

teacherActions.content: A scenario describes a specific situation or process, for example: onboarding a new employee.
teacherActions.label: Scenario definition

teacherActions.content: Next, use the workflow template to document each step, including who is responsible, needed actions, inputs, and expected outcomes.
teacherActions.label: Highlight template

teacherActions.content: Finally, review and refine your workflow pack to ensure all steps are clear and responsibilities are assigned.
teacherActions.label: Highlight review

teacherActions.content: A workflow pack is a set of instructions ready for pilot testing in your organization.
teacherActions.label: Define workflow pack

actions.text: Let's walk through how to create your own pilot workflow pack using these three steps.
First, select a real-world scenario from your workplace. This could be a process you'd like to improve or automate.
actions.title: Highlight scenario

actions.text: A scenario describes a specific situation or process, for example: onboarding a new employee.

actions.title: Define workflow pack

questions.question: In workflow automation, which of the following best describes a 'trigger' in a playbook designed for handling customer complaints in a retail store?

options.label: An automated message sent after a complaint is resolved
A specific event, such as a new complaint submission, that starts the automated process
A list of all store locations using automation
The final summary report created at the end of the month

questions.analysis: B is correct because a 'trigger' is the specific event that initiates the workflow, such as when a customer submits a new complaint. The other options describe actions or outputs, not the starting point of automation.

questions.question: Which elements are essential when designing an effective workflow automation playbook for a human resources (HR) onboarding process? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Clearly defined triggers, such as an accepted job offer
A sequence of actions with responsible parties identified
A daily backup of employee records unrelated to onboarding
Conditional steps depending on role or department

questions.analysis: A, B, and D are correct. A workflow playbook for HR onboarding needs clear triggers, defined steps and responsibilities, and conditional actions for various roles. C is incorrect because data backups, while important, are not part of the onboarding workflow design.

actions.text: To wrap up, let's apply what we just covered about designing workflow automation playbooks, especially in practical settings outside traditional IT teams.

For the first question, remember: a 'trigger' is simply the event that starts an automated workflow. For example, in a retail store complaint process, the trigger could be a new complaint being submitted, which sets the automation in motion.

The second question highlights essential ingredients in an HR onboarding workflow playbook. Look for options that clearly outline what starts the process, specify who is responsible for each action, and include any steps that might change depending on the job role or department. Avoid picking actions unrelated to onboarding, like daily backups.

Both questions focus on the importance of identifying the correct triggers and building logical, scenario-based workflows that anyone can follow. Setting clear starting points and responsibilities makes automations reliable and effective.

Congratulations on completing the course! You've built a solid foundation for automating workflows and applying agentic AI in real-world business scenarios. Take these principles into your own workplace processes—clear triggers, defined steps, and scenario-based planning will serve you well. Thank you for your engaged participation.
